demos.question: What approvals and documentation do I need to ensure are in place before I can process an international travel request for a staff member who has a pre-existing medical condition?
demos.trajectory:
[[ ## thought_0 ## ]]
The user is asking about the required approvals and documentation for an international travel request, specifically for a staff member with a pre-existing medical condition.

I need to break this down into two key areas of research:
1.  The standard process for international travel requests (approvals, documentation).
2.  Specific procedures or requirements related to pre-existing medical conditions for traveling staff.

I will start by searching for general policies on international travel to get a baseline understanding. A `semantic_metadata_search` is a good starting point as it will search titles and descriptions, which should quickly identify the main travel policy documents.

[[ ## tool_name_0 ## ]]
semantic_metadata_search

[[ ## tool_args_0 ## ]]
{"query": "international travel request approvals and documentation"}

[[ ## observation_0 ## ]]
[1] «International Agreements Procedure»
[2] «Dual Purpose Travel (DPT) Procedure»
[3] «Travel Policy»
[4] «International Student Supervision Program Procedure»
[5] «International Agreements Guideline»

[[ ## thought_1 ## ]]
The initial search has identified "Travel Policy" as the most likely primary document for general international travel procedures. Now I need to address the second, more specific part of the user's question: requirements for staff with a pre-existing medical condition. The first search didn't yield any titles that obviously relate to medical conditions. Therefore, I need to conduct a more focused search on this topic. A `semantic_content_search` will be more effective as it can find relevant passages inside documents that might not be explicitly titled "Medical Conditions for Travel".

[[ ## tool_name_1 ## ]]
semantic_content_search

[[ ## tool_args_1 ## ]]
{"query": "travel for staff with pre-existing medical condition"}

[[ ## observation_1 ## ]]
Source: Travel Policy
Content: #### International Travel - Fitness for Travel Requirement

(36)  If a UQ traveller is planning to undertake international UQ travel and has a pre-existing condition, or if the UQ traveller is over 75 years of age, they must obtain medical clearance to travel from a doctor within 30 days prior to the UQ travel using the Fit for Travel form available on the [UQ Insurance Services website](https://policies.uq.edu.au/download.php?id=1042&version=5&associated).

(37)  A pre-existing condition is a physical defect, condition, illness or disease for which treatment, medication or advice (including advice for treatment) has been received or prescribed by a doctor in the 12 months prior to the UQ travel. This also includes pregnancy and any mental illness.

(38)  The Fit for Travel form must be completed by UQ travellers prior to booking their international UQ travel if, at the time, they are showing symptoms of any defect, condition, illness or disease.

Source: Travel Policy
Content: #### Supervisors

(74)  The UQ traveller's supervisor should confirm the following with the UQ traveller prior to departure:

  1. appropriate controls have been or will be implemented to effectively manage the risks associated with undertaking UQ travel;
  2. the traveller is fit to undertake UQ travel having regard to the nature, purpose, destination, and duration of the travel (it's the responsibility of the traveller to get a medical clearance for international travel if they have a pre-existing condition); and
  3. appropriate communication arrangements have been put in place to remain in contact with the traveller.

Source: Remote or Isolated Work in Research Environments Guideline
Content: (14)  UQ workers with known health conditions that may impact on their own health, safety or wellness, or that of others when performing remote or isolated work in research environments, must be fit to undertake the work, and if required, have a medical management plan from their treating medical practitioner, ensure they have any necessary personal medications with them, and advise their manager or supervisor of this plan.

(15)  Any medical or personal information supplied to the supervisor as evidence of fitness to work (or otherwise) must be confidentially managed by the supervisor in accordance with the [Privacy Policy](https://policies.uq.edu.au/document/view-current.php?id=480) and [Procedure](https://policies.uq.edu.au/document/view-current.php?id=12).

Source: Travel Policy
Content: # Section 3 - Roles, Responsibilities and Accountabilities

### UQ Travellers

(69)  UQ travellers are responsible for complying with applicable sections of this Policy, the [Dual Purpose Travel (DPT) Procedure](https://policies.uq.edu.au/document/view-current.php?id=32) and the [Travel Website](https://policies.uq.edu.au/download.php?id=18&version=2&associated) including:

  1. exploring all viable options to achieve the business objective (e.g. virtual attendance at meetings, conferences, workshops etc.);
  2. ensuring UQ travel is properly budgeted for and funds are available to pay for the travel and all associated costs;
  3. discussing their travel plans with their supervisor prior to submitting a travel request;
  4. planning their travel and obtaining necessary approvals, within a timely manner prior to UQ travel;
  5. ensuring UQ travel is planned and undertaken in the most cost effective and efficient manner;
  6. ensuring their work responsibilities are suitably covered during the UQ travel;
  7. assessing the risks and ensuring appropriate precautions are taken and risk management actions taken to protect themselves from medical, safety, security and related risks including: 
     1. assessing and ensuring their own fitness to travel and obtain medical clearance for international travel from a doctor if required; (UQ will reimburse UQ travellers for the cost of an appointment with a doctor to assess fitness for travel if the UQ traveller has a pre-existing condition or is over 75 years of age);
     2. obtaining necessary vaccinations/medications; (UQ will reimburse UQ travellers for the cost of vaccinations/medications required for their specific UQ travel destinations, other than for vaccinations listed in the Immunise Australia Program);
     3. seeking appropriate medical and security advice from [ISOS](https://policies.uq.edu.au/download.php?id=20&version=1&associated) prior to travel;
     4. completing risk assessment documentation where required;
     5. providing true and complete travel-related information to UQ;
     6. ensuring their contact details and travel itinerary is kept up-to-date with [ISOS](https://policies.uq.edu.au/download.php?id=20&version=1&associated);
     7. promptly responding to UQ or [ISOS](https://policies.uq.edu.au/download.php?id=20&version=1&associated) when contacted; and
     8. establishing appropriate communication arrangements with their supervisor and advising their supervisor of any changes to their itinerary or the risk profile of their travel;
  8. holding a valid passport, appropriate visas and complying with relevant immigration requirements for the entire duration of UQ travel;
  9. checking the accuracy of their travel documentation prior to travel and understanding the terms and conditions of their UQ travel bookings;
  10. managing travel-related costs and expenses responsibly and consistently in accordance with UQ policies and procedures;
  11. identifying any private travel costs during the UQ travel and pay their personal contribution at the time of booking;
  12. disclosing to UQ any relevant details regarding private travel or UQ travel with accompanying non-UQ travellers or an approved non-UQ person to facilitate any audit and reviews undertaken by UQ or a party engaged by UQ;
  13. maintaining and, where appropriate, providing copies of necessary travel-related records. All travel-related records must be finalised within 30 days of return from UQ travel;
  14. ensuring all UQ assets are reasonably protected during UQ travel; and
  15. ensuring that there are no international safeguards (e.g. sanction laws and regulations) under Australian law that may prohibit or restrict certain travel.

Source: Medical Conditions Affecting Performance Procedure
Content: # Section 5 - Medical Advice

(4)  The Case Manager may seek appropriate medical information and advice in order to assess the staff member’s ability or otherwise to perform the full range of duties associated with their position.

(5)  In circumstances where the staff member is confirmed to be medically fit to undertake the full range of duties associated with their position with any reasonable adjustments (as appropriate), any remaining performance concerns will be addressed in accordance with the [Diminished Performance and Unsatisfactory Performance Policy](https://policies.uq.edu.au/document/view-current.php?id=71).

Source: Medical Conditions Affecting Performance Policy
Content: ### Independent Medical Examination

(13)  Where, after thorough investigation (including the processes as outlined in the [Workers' Compensation and Rehabilitation Procedure](https://policies.uq.edu.au/document/view-current.php?id=293)), the capacity of a staff member to perform the duties of their role is in doubt, the relevant Senior Executive may require the staff member, in writing, to undergo medical examination subject to the notice requirements contained in the [Medical Conditions Affecting Performance Procedure](https://policies.uq.edu.au/document/view-current.php?id=262).

Source: Medical Conditions Affecting Performance Policy
Content: This is the current version of the approved document. You can provide feedback on this document to the Enquiries Contact - refer to the Status and Details tab from the menu bar above.  
  
# Section 1 - Purpose and Objectives

(1)  This Policy outlines the provisions related to the management of medical conditions affecting the performance of University staff. This Policy will be applied in accordance with the Enterprise Agreement and the [Medical Conditions Affecting Performance Procedure](https://policies.uq.edu.au/document/view-current.php?id=262).

Source: Work Off-Campus and Fieldwork Procedure
Content: ### Health Conditions Affecting Work Off-campus or Field Work Participation

(34) Work off-campus or field work participants with a health condition that may impact on the health, safety or wellness of themselves or others, must advise their WOC/FW leader of the relevant medical management plan after discussion with a medical practitioner.

Source: Health Surveillance for Laboratory Animal Allergy (LAA) Guideline
Content: # Section 8 - Management of Established Cases

(23) In all cases where symptoms are reported or suspected, the worker will be referred to the OHNA for review. Subsequent action will be taken by workplace management after medical recommendations are received.

(24) Medical recommendations may include:

  1. Further investigation to define the nature and severity of the person’s condition and treatment if appropriate.
  2. Increasing the frequency of medical surveillance to ensure the condition does not worsen and to ensure the condition is managed appropriately.

Source: Medical Conditions Affecting Performance Policy
Content: # Section 5 - Medical Advice

(8)  In order to support and contribute to the provision of rehabilitation for staff whose ability to carry out their duties is affected by an injury or illness, the University may need to consider appropriate medical information and advice.

(9)  Nothing in this Policy or the associated procedures restricts any right the University may have to direct a staff member to attend a medical appointment where there are genuine occupational health and safety reasons for this. Any such direction will operate independently of this Policy and not be part of the associated procedures.

Source: Work Integrated Learning and Work Experience Procedure
Content: ### Compliance

(16)  If a student undertakes travel in relation to their work-related learning opportunity, they must:

  1. adhere to the [Travel Policy](https://policies.uq.edu.au/document/view-current.php?id=8) as it relates to their activity, and
  2. comply with any laws and regulations applicable to their WIL or Work Experience opportunity in the relevant jurisdiction.

### Occupational Health and Safety (OHS)

(17)  UQ will take steps to ensure that the Host Organisation and any relevant Third-Party Provider has appropriate OHS risk management systems and strategies in place. UQ’s standard agreement for WIL and/or Work Experience outlines the obligations and expectations of the Host Organisation.

Source: Medical Conditions Affecting Performance Policy
Content: # Section 4 - Policy Statement

(3)  The University is committed to accident prevention and health promotion. However, the University recognises that despite its best efforts, some medical conditions can arise (i.e. injuries and illnesses) that affect a staff member's ability to carry out their duties. These injuries and illnesses can arise at the workplace or outside.

(4)  The University has an obligation to support and contribute to the provision of rehabilitation for all staff whose ability to carry out their duties is affected by injury or illness. The [Workers' Compensation and Rehabilitation Policy](https://policies.uq.edu.au/document/view-current.php?id=107) and [Workers' Compensation and Rehabilitation Procedure](https://policies.uq.edu.au/document/view-current.php?id=293) are administered by the Health, Safety and Wellness Division, and can operate in conjunction with this Policy.
demos.next_thought: I have successfully identified the key policy document, "Travel Policy," which addresses both the general requirements for international travel and the specific procedures for staff with pre-existing medical conditions. The `semantic_content_search` provided detailed excerpts from this policy, confirming the need for a "Fit for Travel form" and outlining the responsibilities of both the traveler and their supervisor. Other documents found, such as the "Medical Conditions Affecting Performance Policy," appear to be less directly relevant to the user's specific question about travel approval. Since I have found the primary document that covers all aspects of the user's query, I have sufficient information to answer the question and can conclude the research phase.

signature.instructions:
Conduct research to find all policy document titles relevant to answering the user's question.

**First, identify all the distinct concepts or topics in the user's query (e.g., different materials, separate procedures, etc.).**

Your research strategy must **ensure you find relevant documents for *each* of these distinct concepts.** A single search for all terms at once may fail to find everything.

Use your tools strategically. Start with precise keywords. **If a combined search doesn't return results for a specific topic, you MUST conduct a new, targeted search for that missing topic individually.**

Be meticulous in your research. Before outputting your list, **verify that your collected titles actually cover all the key topics from the user's question.** Your final output is a list of the most relevant document titles that, together, will help answer the *entire* question.

You are an Agent. In each episode, you will be given the fields `question` as input. And you can see your past trajectory so far.
Your goal is to use one or more of the supplied tools to collect any necessary information for producing `titles`, `notes`.

To do this, you will interleave next_thought, next_tool_name, and next_tool_args in each turn, and also when finishing the task.
After each tool call, you receive a resulting observation, which gets appended to your trajectory.

When writing next_thought, you may reason about the current situation and plan for future steps.
When selecting the next_tool_name and its next_tool_args, the tool must be one of:

(1) semantic_metadata_search, whose description is <desc>Performs a fast semantic search on only the titles and descriptions of documents. Use this as a FIRST STEP to quickly identify a list of potentially relevant policies based on a general topic or concept. Returns a list of document titles and descriptions.</desc>. It takes arguments {'query': {'type': 'string'}}.
(2) semantic_content_search, whose description is <desc>Performs a broad semantic search on the FULL TEXT content of all documents. Use this to find specific facts, procedures, or nuanced details deep inside documents, especially for vague questions where the exact keywords are unknown. Returns relevant text chunks and their source titles.</desc>. It takes arguments {'query': {'type': 'string'}}.
(3) keyword_search, whose description is <desc>Searches the full text of all documents for exact keyword matches. Best for finding specific, non-negotiable terms like official form names, acronyms, or technical terms that semantic search might misinterpret. Use multiple comma-separated keywords to broaden the search and find documents that may not use the exact original term (e.g., 'AI, artificial, intelligence, generative').</desc>. It takes arguments {'query': {'type': 'string'}}.
(4) get_full_document_text, whose description is <desc>Retrieves the COMPLETE, unabridged text of a single document given its exact title. Use this only AFTER you have identified a highly relevant document title from one of your search tools to gain a comprehensive understanding.</desc>. It takes arguments {'title': {'type': 'string'}}.
(5) finish, whose description is <desc>Marks the task as complete. That is, signals that all information for producing the outputs, i.e. `titles`, `notes`, are now available to be extracted.</desc>. It takes arguments {}.
When providing `next_tool_args`, the value inside the field must be in JSON format

My research strategy addressed both of these components.

demos.question: What is the annual requirement for Academic Staff and Nominated Senior Staff regarding foreign influence disclosures, and what happens if their disclosure requires registration?

[[ ## observation_1 ## ]]
Source: Foreign Influence Disclosure Procedure
Content: This is the current version of the approved document. You can provide feedback on this document to the Enquiries Contact - refer to the Status and Details tab from the menu bar above. To view historical versions, click the link in the document's navigation bar.  
  
# Section 1 - Purpose and Scope

(1)  The [Foreign Influence Transparency Scheme Act 2018](https://policies.uq.edu.au/directory-summary.php?legislation=286) ([FITSA](https://policies.uq.edu.au/directory-summary.php?legislation=286)) imposes registration obligations for individuals and entities that undertake a registrable activity on behalf of a foreign principal, or enter into a registrable arrangement with a foreign principal. The scheme, established under the FITSA, aims to improve transparency with respect to foreign influence over Australia’s political and government processes.

(2)  Under this Procedure, UQ staff members are required to disclose any activities which may give rise to a requirement to register, for the purpose of ensuring compliance with the FITSA.

Source: Foreign Influence Disclosure Procedure
Content: # Section 2 - Process and Key Controls

(9)  Academic Staff and Nominated Senior Staff are required to disclose registrable activities through UQ's [Foreign Influence Disclosure](https://policies.uq.edu.au/download.php?id=1153&version=3&associated) tool (the 'Disclosure'), though the tool is accessible to all UQ staff.

(10)  Disclosures made by Academic Staff and Nominated Senior Staff through UQ’s Foreign Influence Disclosure will be evaluated on a case-by-case basis by the Deputy Provost. The Deputy Provost may seek advice from relevant Senior Executives, including UQ’s General Counsel (or delegate) and consult with the Chief Human Resources Officer (or delegate) and/or the Provost as part of the evaluation process.

(11)  If registration is required, this will be done by UQ externally through the [Foreign Influence Transparency Scheme Public Register](https://policies.uq.edu.au/download.php?id=999&version=2&associated) (the ‘Transparency Register’) housed on the Attorney-General’s website.

Source: Foreign Influence Disclosure Procedure
Content: * The term Foreign Influence Transparency Scheme is defined as: According to FITSA (2018), the scheme “establishes registration obligations for individuals and entities that undertake certain activities on behalf of foreign principals. Whether or not a person is required to register under the scheme depends on who the foreign principal is, the nature of the activities undertaken, the purpose for which the activities are undertaken and, in some cases, whether the person has held a senior public position in Australia.”
  * The term [Foreign Influence Transparency Scheme Act 2018](https://policies.uq.edu.au/directory-summary.php?legislation=286) is defined as: According to FITSA (2018), “An Act to establish a scheme to improve the transparency of activities undertaken on behalf of foreign principals, and for related purposes.”
  * The term Foreign Influence Disclosure is defined as: UQ’s online process for establishing registrable activities and recording whether these have been registered on the Commonwealth Government’s [Transparency Register](https://policies.uq.edu.au/download.php?id=999&version=2&associated).

Source: Foreign Influence Disclosure Procedure
Content: ### Heads of Organisational Unit

(20)  Heads of Organisational Unit are responsible for the compliance by their Academic Staff and Nominated Senior Staff with this Procedure.

### Academic Staff and Nominated Senior Staff

(21)  Academic Staff and Nominated Senior Staff are responsible for:

  1. completing UQ’s [Foreign Influence Disclosure](https://policies.uq.edu.au/download.php?id=1153&version=3&associated) (even if no activities are disclosed), disclosing their activities on the Disclosure, and updating where circumstances change;
  2. providing all relevant and up to date information and documents to support any registration; and
  3. complying with all ongoing registration obligations in relation to registrable activities recorded on the [Transparency Register](https://policies.uq.edu.au/download.php?id=999&version=2&associated).

Source: Conflict of Interest for Members of Staff Procedure
Content: ### Disclosure and Management of Interests

(21)  The [Conflict of Interest Policy](https://policies.uq.edu.au/document/view-current.php?id=68) and associated procedures is part of a framework for the governance and management of public and private interests across a number of related areas of activity.

(22)  The disclosure and management of these interests requires relevant staff to complete the following:

  1. Conflict of Interest Disclosure Tool
  2. Secondary Employment Register
  3. Sensitive Research Register
  4. Foreign Influence and Interference Register.

(23)  To complete the Foreign Influence and Interference Register, staff are required to enter data into the Individual Activity Profile that relates to supervision of students at foreign institutions and the holding of honorary/adjunct appointments at foreign institutions.

Source: Foreign Influence Disclosure Procedure
Content: ### Resources Provided by External Entities

(26)  The Department of the Attorney-General provides a pre-registration questionnaire to determine eligibility for external registration:

  1. [Transparency Portal](https://policies.uq.edu.au/download.php?id=1490&version=1&associated).

(27)  The Department of the Attorney-General provides the Foreign Influence Transparency Scheme Public Register:

  1. [Transparency Register](https://policies.uq.edu.au/download.php?id=999&version=2&associated).

(28)  The Department of the Attorney-General also provides:

  1. [Foreign Influence Transparency Scheme Information Page](https://policies.uq.edu.au/download.php?id=1000&version=1&associated).
  2. [Foreign Influence Transparency Scheme Resources](https://policies.uq.edu.au/download.php?id=1001&version=3&associated).

(29)  The Federal Register of Legislation provides access to:

  1. [Foreign Influence Transparency Scheme Act 2018](https://policies.uq.edu.au/directory-summary.php?legislation=286) (Cth).

Source: Foreign Influence Disclosure Procedure
Content: (12)  Academic Staff and Nominated Senior Staff whose disclosures are assessed as registrable in the Transparency Register:

  1. Will undertake relevant processes for disclosure and registration as set out in the ‘Disclosure and Registration Requirements’ provisions below (Section 3);
  2. Must not commence the registrable activity until they receive advice from UQ that the registration has been completed; and
  3. Must ensure the ongoing registration obligations are adhered to, which is a requirement under the [Conflict of Interest for Members of Staff Procedure](https://policies.uq.edu.au/document/view-current.php?id=204).

(13)  The conduct of staff in relation to this Procedure, including potential breaches of the Procedure, will be dealt with in accordance with [Staff Code of Conduct Policy](https://policies.uq.edu.au/document/view-current.php?id=91). The conduct of Staff in relation to research will be dealt with in accordance with [Managing Complaints about the Conduct of Research Procedure](https://policies.uq.edu.au/document/view-current.php?id=352).

Source: International Agreements Policy
Content: # Section 6 - Appendix

### Definitions, Terms and Acronyms

  * The term International Agreement is defined as: Contracts or agreements with a foreign entity, including foreign: (i) Universities, higher education or research institutions; (ii) Governments and their agencies; and (iii) Sponsors, scholarship providers and corporations, that document intended or required co-operation and other activities by the University and the foreign entity that are: • Contracts and Agreements about: (i) Undergraduate and postgraduate student exchange and study abroad; (ii) Higher degree research supervision and mobility; (iii) Academic and professional staff mobility; (iv) Pathway articulation; (v) International scholarship and sponsorship; and (vi) General academic and research collaboration, provided that: (a) 'research collaboration’ agreement above, means an overarching, non-binding agreement or arrangement that does require the, payment or receipt of money or other valuable consideration or the actual undertaking of research; and (b) International Agreements exclude Research Agreements made through the Deputy Vice-Chancellor (Research and Innovation)'s Organisational Area.
  * The term Engagement Activity/Initiative is defined as: Includes collaborative academic programs, general cooperative research, student and staff mobility and visits and joint academic events.
  * The term International Partners is defined as: Includes offshore higher education institutions, research institutions, public bodies, foreign governments, sponsors, scholarship providers, agencies and corporations.

Source: International Agreements Procedure
Content: # Section 5 - Monitoring, Review and Assurance

(32) The Pro-Vice-Chancellor (Global Partnerships) is responsible for the implementation of this Procedure, including ensuring that Executive Deans, Institute Directors and other Division leaders report on their international engagement activities as required under this Procedure.

(33) The International Agreements team will review this Procedure as required to ensure its currency and relevance to the management of international engagement activities at UQ.

Source: International Agreements Procedure
Content: # Section 1 - Purpose and Scope

(1) This Procedure outlines the processes required for establishing and managing partnership agreements with International Partners.

(2) This Procedure supports the [International Agreements Policy](https://policies.uq.edu.au/document/view-current.php?id=157) and covers agreements that establish, document and support collaborative relationships and activities with International Partners (i.e. governments, institutions, and business), and includes undergraduate, postgraduate and higher degree research student and staff mobility programs, pathway articulation programs, sponsorship/scholarship arrangements and agreements that support general research collaborations with International Partners (e.g. International Cooperation Agreements, Memoranda of Understanding, Memoranda of Agreement, Letters of Intent, etc).

(3) This Procedure applies to UQ staff involved in:

  1. the development of International Agreement proposals; and
  2. the ongoing management of International Agreements.

Source: International Agreements Procedure
Content: # Section 3 - Key Requirements

### International Agreement proposal

(11) Faculties and Institutes seeking to establish an International Agreement must develop a written proposal using a [Proposal Form for a New International Agreement](https://policies.uq.edu.au/download.php?id=156&version=4&associated). The proposal must:

  1. show how the proposed collaborative activity aligns with UQ’s strategic priorities and [Global Engagement Framework](https://policies.uq.edu.au/download.php?id=1465&version=5&associated);
  2. include due diligence of the International Partner with support from Global Partnerships, Research Partnerships and other relevant areas;
  3. include appropriate business planning and risk management measures commensurate to the type of engagement;
  4. identify expected outcomes of the activities and how success will be measured;
  5. confirm that consultation that has been undertaken with relevant stakeholders across UQ; and
  6. identify either an individual or a management team within UQ that will be responsible for managing the activities of the agreement.

Source: International Agreements Procedure
Content: (12) Additional consultation and approvals may be required for certain types of agreements, including Student Mobility, Recognition of Prior Learning (Articulation, Credit Transfer), Double Degrees, Joint Awards, Scholarships, and agreements relating to Transnational Education.

(13) Descriptions of these agreements are provided in the appendix (section 7).

(14) Further information about additional consultation and approvals is provided in the [International Agreements Guideline](https://policies.uq.edu.au/document/view-current.php?id=414).

(15) Faculties, Institutes and Divisions should contact [Global Partnerships](https://policies.uq.edu.au/download.php?id=420&version=3&associated) for assistance and advice in developing their proposals, including more detailed information about their proposal’s consultation and approval obligations.

Source: Foreign Influence Disclosure Procedure
Content: ### Disclosure and Registration Requirements

(16)  Academic Staff and Nominated Senior Staff must complete the Disclosure (internal registration) on an annual basis. If a disclosure is evaluated as requiring registration on the [Transparency Register](https://policies.uq.edu.au/download.php?id=999&version=2&associated), the University will apply for registration on the staff member’s behalf. In accordance with the FITSA, the period for registration on the Transparency Register is 14 days.

demos.reasoning:
The user's question has two distinct parts: the annual requirement for foreign influence disclosures for specific staff types, and the process if that disclosure requires registration.

My initial search for "foreign influence disclosure requirements" immediately identified the "Foreign Influence Disclosure Procedure" as the most relevant document.

A subsequent content search within this document confirmed that it contains the answers to both parts of the user's question. One text snippet explicitly states that "Academic Staff and Nominated Senior Staff must complete the Disclosure (internal registration) on an annual basis." Other snippets detail the process that follows a disclosure, explaining that if registration is deemed necessary, "the University will apply for registration on the staff member’s behalf" on the external "Transparency Register".

Since this single document comprehensively addresses all aspects of the user's query, no further research is needed.
demos.notes: The "Foreign Influence Disclosure Procedure" is the key document. It specifies that the disclosure is an internal process, and if external registration is required under the Foreign Influence Transparency Scheme Act 2018 (FITSA), the university handles the registration on behalf of the staff member.
